Named after Albert Einstein! First found in nuclear test debris.
Einsteinium was discovered in the radioactive cloud from the world's first hydrogen bomb test in 1952 — but kept secret for years!
Doesn't exist naturally. Made in tiny amounts in special reactors.
Scientific research only
The 100th element! Named after the famous physicist Enrico Fermi.
Fermium was also discovered in the same hydrogen bomb test debris as einsteinium — that one explosion gave us TWO new elements!
Made in nuclear reactors. Only billionths of a gram have ever been produced.
Scientific research only
Named after Mendeleev, the guy who invented the periodic table!
Mendeleev created the periodic table in 1869 by predicting elements that didn't even exist yet — and he was right about almost all of them!
Doesn't exist naturally. Made one atom at a time in particle accelerators.
Scientific research only
Named after Alfred Nobel, who created the Nobel Prize and dynamite!
Alfred Nobel invented dynamite, made a fortune, then felt bad about it and used his money to create the Nobel Prizes — pretty cool legacy!
Doesn't exist naturally. Made in particle accelerators.
Scientific research only
Named after Ernest Lawrence, who invented the cyclotron particle smasher!
The cyclotron Lawrence invented is what we use to MAKE elements like lawrencium — the tool created the element that's named after its inventor!
Doesn't exist naturally. Made in particle accelerators.
Scientific research only
Super heavy, super radioactive, and only exists for tiny fractions of a second!
Some atoms of rutherfordium last only seconds before splitting apart — scientists have to study them really, really fast!
Made one atom at a time in particle accelerators in Russia and the United States.
Scientific research only
Named after Dubna, Russia, where it was made in a giant atom smasher!
Russian and American scientists fought for decades over who discovered dubnium first — they finally compromised on the name in 1997!
Made in particle accelerators in Dubna, Russia.
Scientific research only
Named after Glenn Seaborg, who was alive when the element was named after him!
Most elements are named after dead scientists — Seaborg was one of the only people to have an element named after him while still living!
Made in particle accelerators by smashing atoms together at high speeds.
Scientific research only
Named after physicist Niels Bohr, who figured out how atoms are built!
Niels Bohr figured out how electrons orbit atoms way back in 1913 — every atom diagram you'll ever see uses his model!
Made by smashing chromium atoms into bismuth at high speed.
Scientific research only
Named after Hesse, the German state where it was first made!
Scientists have only ever made a few hundred atoms of hassium — total — in the entire history of humanity!
Made in particle accelerators in Darmstadt, Germany.
Scientific research only
Named after Lise Meitner, who helped discover nuclear fission!
Lise Meitner discovered nuclear fission but didn't get the Nobel Prize for it — her partner did! She finally got an element named after her instead.
Made in particle accelerators in Germany.
Scientific research only
Made by smashing nickel into lead inside a particle accelerator!
To make darmstadtium, scientists fired nickel atoms at lead atoms a billion billion times — and only got one new atom for their trouble!
Made at the GSI lab in Darmstadt, Germany.
Scientific research only
Named after Wilhelm Röntgen, who discovered X-rays!
Röntgen accidentally discovered X-rays in 1895 while playing with a tube — the first X-ray photo he took was of his wife's hand showing her bones and ring!
Made in particle accelerators in Germany.
Scientific research only
Named after astronomer Copernicus, who said Earth orbits the Sun!
In Copernicus's time, people thought the Sun went around Earth — he was so worried about getting in trouble that he waited until he was dying to publish his theory!
Made in particle accelerators by smashing zinc into lead.
Scientific research only
The first element discovered in Asia! 'Nihon' means Japan in Japanese.
It took Japanese scientists 9 years and they only made 3 atoms of nihonium total — but it was enough to officially get naming rights!
Made at the RIKEN lab in Japan.
Scientific research only
Named after a Russian scientist who studied super-heavy atoms!
Flerovium is one of the most stable super-heavy elements — its atoms can last for several seconds, which is forever in this part of the periodic table!
Made in Dubna, Russia, by smashing calcium into plutonium.
Scientific research only
Named after Moscow, Russia, where it was created!
There's a wild conspiracy theory that aliens use moscovium for spaceship fuel — there's zero evidence for it, but it's a fun sci-fi idea!
Made in Dubna, Russia.
Scientific research only
Named after the Lawrence Livermore Lab in California!
Livermorium atoms exist for less than a millisecond before decaying — scientists basically blink and miss them!
Made by Russian and American scientists working together in Dubna.
Scientific research only
Named after Tennessee! One of the newest elements ever made.
To make tennessine, scientists in Russia needed berkelium from Tennessee — they had to ship the precious material 7,000 miles to make a brand new element!
Made by smashing calcium into berkelium at the lab in Dubna, Russia.
Scientific research only
The heaviest element known! Named after a still-living scientist, Yuri Oganessian.
Even though it's in the noble gas column, oganesson might actually be a SOLID at room temperature — but it decays so fast nobody can really test it!
Made in Dubna, Russia. Only a handful of atoms have ever existed.
Scientific research only
Atom
The tiniest piece of matter that still acts like a specific element. Everything around you — air, water, your body — is made of atoms!
A gold ring is made of billions of gold atoms.
Atomic Number
The number of protons inside an atom's nucleus. It tells you which element it is and where it sits on the periodic table.
Hydrogen is #1 (1 proton), Helium is #2 (2 protons).
Atomic Mass
How heavy an atom is compared to other atoms. It counts both protons and neutrons together.
Carbon has an atomic mass of about 12.
Element
A pure substance made of only one kind of atom. There are 118 known elements, and they're all on the periodic table!
Gold, oxygen, and carbon are all elements.
Molecule
Two or more atoms bonded together. A molecule can be made of the same element or different elements.
Water (H₂O) is a molecule of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom.
Proton
A tiny particle inside the nucleus of an atom with a positive electric charge. The number of protons defines which element it is.
Every carbon atom has exactly 6 protons.
Neutron
A particle inside the nucleus with no electric charge. It helps hold the nucleus together.
Carbon usually has 6 neutrons alongside its 6 protons.
Electron
A super tiny particle that zooms around the nucleus with a negative charge. Electrons are what make electricity and chemical reactions happen.
When you get a static shock, electrons are jumping between surfaces!
Nucleus
The dense center of an atom where protons and neutrons live. It's tiny compared to the whole atom but contains almost all of its mass.
If an atom were a stadium, the nucleus would be a pea on the field.
Periodic Table
A chart that organizes all known elements by their atomic number and properties. Elements in the same column tend to behave similarly.
Mendeleev created the first version in 1869!
Group (Column)
A vertical column on the periodic table. Elements in the same group share similar chemical properties.
Group 1 elements (like sodium and potassium) all react explosively with water.
Period (Row)
A horizontal row on the periodic table. As you move across a period, elements change from metals to nonmetals.
Period 2 goes from lithium (a metal) to neon (a gas).
Alkali Metal
Elements in the first column of the periodic table. They are soft, shiny metals that react violently with water!
Sodium and potassium are alkali metals — drop them in water and boom!
Alkaline Earth Metal
Elements in the second column. They are harder than alkali metals and also reactive, but not as dramatically.
Calcium (in your bones) and magnesium (in plants) are alkaline earth metals.
Transition Metal
The big block of metals in the middle of the periodic table. They are strong, shiny, and great at conducting heat and electricity.
Iron, copper, gold, and silver are all transition metals.
Post-Transition Metal
Metals that sit to the right of the transition metals. They are softer and melt at lower temperatures.
Aluminum (soda cans) and tin (tin cans) are post-transition metals.
Metalloid
Elements that have properties of both metals and nonmetals — they're in between! Great for electronics.
Silicon is a metalloid that powers every computer chip.
Nonmetal
Elements that are NOT shiny or good conductors. Many are gases at room temperature. They're essential for life!
Oxygen, carbon, and nitrogen are all nonmetals.
Halogen
A group of very reactive nonmetals. They love to grab electrons from other elements to form salts.
Chlorine (pool cleaner) and fluorine (in toothpaste) are halogens.
Noble Gas
The most chill elements — they almost never react with anything because their electron shells are already full.
Helium, neon, and argon are noble gases. Neon makes those glowing signs!
Lanthanide
A row of 15 rare-earth metals. Despite the name, they're not always that rare — they are used in tech like phone screens and magnets.
Neodymium makes the super-strong magnets in headphones.
Actinide
A row of 15 elements, most of which are radioactive. They include the fuels for nuclear power.
Uranium and plutonium are actinides used in nuclear energy.
State of Matter
Whether something is a solid, liquid, or gas. Most elements are solid at room temperature, but some are gases or even liquids!
Mercury and bromine are the only elements that are liquid at room temperature.
Chemical Bond
The 'glue' that holds atoms together to form molecules. Atoms share or trade electrons to bond.
In water (H₂O), oxygen shares electrons with two hydrogen atoms.
Chemical Reaction
When atoms rearrange to form new substances. You can't create or destroy atoms — they just switch partners!
Burning wood is a chemical reaction — carbon and oxygen combine to make CO₂.
Isotope
Atoms of the same element that have different numbers of neutrons. Same element, slightly different weight!
Carbon-12 and Carbon-14 are both carbon, but C-14 is used to date ancient objects.
Radioactive
When an atom's nucleus is unstable and breaks apart over time, releasing energy. Some elements are naturally radioactive.
Uranium is radioactive — it slowly breaks down and releases energy for power plants.
Electron Shell
Layers around the nucleus where electrons live. Each shell can hold a limited number of electrons.
The first shell holds up to 2 electrons, the second holds up to 8.
Ion
An atom that has gained or lost electrons, giving it an electric charge. Positive ions lost electrons, negative ions gained them.
Table salt (NaCl) is made of sodium ions (+) and chloride ions (−).
Alloy
A mixture of two or more metals melted together to make something stronger or more useful.
Steel is an alloy of iron and carbon. Bronze is copper and tin.
Catalyst
A substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being used up itself. It's like a helper that doesn't get tired.
Catalytic converters in cars use platinum to clean exhaust fumes.
Crystal
A solid where atoms are arranged in a neat, repeating pattern. This gives crystals their beautiful shapes.
Diamonds, snowflakes, and table salt are all crystals.
Electrolyte
A substance that conducts electricity when dissolved in water because it breaks into ions.
Sports drinks contain electrolytes like sodium and potassium.
Magnetic
The ability of a material to attract iron and certain other metals. Only a few elements are naturally magnetic.
Iron, cobalt, and nickel are the three naturally magnetic elements.
Oxidation
When an element reacts with oxygen. Rusting and burning are both types of oxidation!
When iron rusts, it's slowly combining with oxygen from the air.
About 99% of your body mass is just 6 elements: oxygen, carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, calcium, and phosphorus.
You are about 60% water — that means most of you is hydrogen and oxygen atoms!
Your body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.
You have enough iron in your body to make a small nail (about 3-4 grams).
Your body generates about 100 watts of electricity — enough to power a light bulb!
Almost every element in your body was forged inside a star billions of years ago. You are literally made of stardust!
Tooth enamel (made of calcium and phosphorus) is the hardest substance your body produces.
Your body contains enough potassium to fire a toy cap gun.
